data.title: Graid Technology Launches Agentic AI Storage Portfolio to Eliminate KV Cache Bottlenecks
data.description:
SUNNYVALE, CA, Apr 22, 2026 - (ACN Newswire) - Graid Technology, the pioneer in GPU-accelerated NVMe storage, today announced its Agentic AI Storage Portfolio: a purpose-built family of KV cache solutions designed to eliminate the storage bottleneck that stalls "always-on" production AI. The portfolio spans three deployment tiers: KV Cache Server, KV Cache Rack, and KV Cache Platform, all built on SupremeRAID(TM) technology. KV Cache Platform, the portfolio's highest tier, is purpose-aligned to NVIDIA's STX reference architecture, with native BlueField-4 DPU execution on the roadmap for H2 2026.

As agentic AI moves from experimentation to production, the infrastructure assumptions that underpinned single-shot inference have broken down. Models running continuous multi-step tasks and maintaining context across hours of operation generate KV cache demands that overwhelm GPU HBM. The result: latency spikes up to 18x, GPU utilization as low as 50%, and model-level failures, including hallucinations and reasoning degradation, that are difficult to detect and costly to recover from.

SupremeRAID addresses this directly, aggregating up to 32 NVMe drives into a single 280 GB/s virtual pool, bypassing the CPU via GPU Direct Storage, and delivering KV cache reads at 1.3ms- 77x faster than standard NVMe. The three portfolio tiers bring this capability to every deployment scale:

KV Cache Server - single-node NVMe acceleration for individual inference servers and edge AI deployments. Available now.

KV Cache Rack - rack-scale, partner-validated solutions co-engineered with leading server OEM partners for enterprise multi-GPU clusters. Available now.

KV Cache Platform - Purpose-built for NVIDIA's STX reference architecture, with native BlueField-4 DPU execution and rack-scale storage expansion on the roadmap.

"A year ago, at GTC 2025, Jensen Huang predicted that storage would become GPU-accelerated for the first time. This year, NVIDIA turned that concept into an architecture with STX and CMX," said Leander Yu, CEO of Graid Technology. "Our KV Cache Portfolio is built for precisely this moment, delivering the storage performance that agentic AI demands, at storage-tier economics."

For enterprises and infrastructure teams evaluating agentic AI deployments, the full deployment architecture, technical specifications, and NVIDIA STX compatibility details are available in the solution brief: Graid Technology Agentic AI Storage Portfolio: Purpose-built KV Cache Solutions for Inference at Scale.

To learn more about Graid Technology's AI offerings, visit graidtech.com/ai.

Media Inquiries:

Andrea Eaken, Sr. Director of Marketing, Americas & EMEA

andrea.eaken@graidtech.com

About Graid Technology

Graid Technology is building the storage backbone for the future of AI, enterprise, and high-performance computing. As the creator of SupremeRAID, the world's first and only GPU-based RAID, and the global steward of Intel Virtual RAID on CPU (Intel VROC), Graid Technology delivers flexible RAID solutions that maximize NVMe performance while ensuring resilient, scalable data protection for modern data infrastructure. Headquartered in Silicon Valley with global operations and R&D in Taiwan, Graid Technology is advancing RAID innovation for the next generation of data-intensive workloads. To learn more, visit graidtech.com.

SOURCE: Graid Technology Inc.

Source: Graid Technology

Copyright 2026 ACN Newswire . All rights reserved.

data.title: Oil Drives Cotton Prices to a Two-Year High — WSJ
data.description:
By Ryan Dezember

Cotton futures notched a two-year high Tuesday of 81 cents a pound and now have risen more than 20% since the U.S. attacked Iran in late February and sent oil prices surging.

Cotton prices are tethered to those of oil because petroleum is the base ingredient of synthetic fibers, such as polyester. When synthetics become expensive, clothing and textile makers turn to cotton, prices for which have been jolted from a two-year slump. U.S. crude futures ended Tuesday 2.8% higher at $92.13 a barrel, or 37% more than before the fighting.

There are other factors fueling cotton's climb as well. Global planted acreage is down and it's been dry in Brazil as well as in parts of the U.S. cotton patch.

U.S. farmers planted slightly more acres this year with cotton than last, yet the footprint of the country's cotton fields is about 16% smaller than in 2024. Meanwhile, there are concerns that yields will be lower because of farmers cutting back on fertilizer, given how prices for plant food have surged due to shipments blocked by fighting in the Persian Gulf.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Amazon.com to Offer Program for GLP-1 Weight-Loss Drugs
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Amazon.com is offering a GLP-1 weight-loss program that integrates primary-care services with the technology company's pharmacy business.

The company said Tuesday the new program is designed to provide continuing medical supervision--allowing clinicians to monitor progress, adjust treatments and address related health conditions--compared with one-off weight-management solutions from other providers.

With the program, Amazon One Medical members can get Novo Nordisk's Wegovy and Eli Lilly's Foundayo GLP-1 pills for weight management starting at $25 a month with insurance coverage, or $149 a month for cash-pay options.

The $149 cash-pay price for a starter dose aligns with those offered through programs from the likes of Hims & Hers Health, Walgreens and Weight Watchers. However, required membership and visit fees can vary. Amazon One Medical costs $99 a year for Prime members.

Amazon Pharmacy also provides injectable versions of Wegovy and Lilly's Zepbound. It currently offers same-day delivery for all medications to nearly 3,000 cities and towns, and said it plans to expand to nearly 4,500 by the end of 2026.

"By expanding access to the latest GLP-1 medications with upfront, clear pricing, we're making it easier for customers to get the treatments their health care providers prescribe and to stay on those medications because they are delivered reliably directly to patients," said Tanvi Patel, vice president and general manager of Amazon Pharmacy.

Shares of Hims & Hers fell 3.9%, to $29.79, on Tuesday. Weight Watchers declined 9%, and Novo Nordisk fell 2.6%.

Under Amazon's new GLP-1 program, patients would receive pre-visit screening, consultations and follow-up visits with integrated monitoring, standardized documentation and treatment algorithms. It is available at Amazon One Medical locations across the U.S., the company said.

Patients enrolled in the program would also be able to get prescription renewals through on-demand telehealth services, starting at $29 for message consultations or $49 for video care.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Hogs Climb as Pork Margins Stay Favorable — Market Talk
data.description: Lean hog futures settle 1.3% higher at $1.0305 a pound. Lean hog futures benefit from strong margins for pork, which are running above seasonal norms, according to a note from Terrain Ag. This in part is due to cheap feed costs, although the ongoing conflict in the Middle East may continue to raise grain prices and weigh on margins for pork. "Producers should consider the risk of feed costs increasing if the conflict in the Middle East continues," the firm says. Also pressing margins is strong demand, coming from higher consumption of pork in cold storage, as well as export sales of pork. Live cattle futures settle down 1.1% to $2.435 a pound. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: The One Bond Alternative You Should Consider. It Isn't What You Think. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Allan Roth

Lately, I'm hearing a lot of people bashing bonds and recommending alternatives to them. The logic is that the growing debt and deficit will cause interest rates to surge. The Fed will have to issue more Treasury bonds to fund the deficit and pay the interest on the ever-increasing debt. And let's face it, no politician has any incentive to tackle the deficit because the only solution is to either increase taxes, cut benefits, or both.

And let's not forget the pain of 2022 where rates surged and the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index lost 13% at the same time the stock market bear came out of hibernation. The so-called shock absorber from bonds hardly provided cushion.

So here are the three bond alternatives I often hear discussed by financial advisors, as well as what I tell my clients when they ask me about them.

Low-Volatility Stocks. The argument goes that certain stocks can act as "bond proxies" because they are mature companies with steady cash flows and higher dividend yields, similar to fixed-income investments. In the past, they have declined less than the market during selloffs, allowing for faster recovery, providing relatively steady cash flows and often better returns than the stock market as a whole.

If this sounds too good to be true, it likely is. I'd love to get stock-like returns with bond-like risk. Bonds are business contracts that are enforceable by law to pay specified numbers of dollars on specified days. Low-volatility stocks are just stocks that have had low volatility in the past. They don't represent any kind of promise by the stock-issuing company. Volatility levels can change for any number of reasons.

Annuities. A single premium immediate annuity (SPIA) is financial contract between the policyholder and an insurance company that turns a lump sum of money into a guaranteed stream of income. Often described as a "personal pension," it is primarily used by retirees to ensure they have a stable, predictable monthly paycheck that they cannot outlive.

As wonderful as this sounds, it does the opposite of protecting against the possibility of higher interest rates. It is essentially an indirect bond fund because insurance companies are typically invested over 80% in fixed income. SPIAs have a very long duration for the rest of the annuitant's life. Think an annuity with a fixed cost-of-living adjustment solves the problem? It's a bit counterintuitive, but because it increases the duration of the payments, it actually increases interest-rate risk.

To illustrate the extreme interest-rate risk of a SPIA, a SPIA purchased in 2021 paid about 25% less than one bought at the end of 2022, after interest rates surged. For investors counting on income, that's roughly equivalent to the 27% loss the Vanguard Long-Term Bond ETF suffered in 2022.

Paying off debt. When you buy a bond, you are lending money to a government or corporation. When you take out a loan, you are borrowing money from a corporation. Thus, the loan can be seen as the inverse of a bond. The logic here is that if you can't earn the same amount on a bond as you are paying on your debt (both after taxes), then you should pay the debt down instead of using that money to buy a bond.

Not only do I agree with that logic, but since the beginning of my practice, I show clients' debt as a negative bond in their portfolio. The decision to pay it off is often a no-brainer if the client is paying more on debt than I can get them on a high-quality bond, both after taxes. Of course, clients must feel comfortable that they have enough liquidity because they typically can't get the money back once they pay off a debt. High-quality bonds, on the other hand, are very liquid and easy to convert to cash when held in a taxable account.

Yes, bonds are boring. Stocks are exciting — even when held as part of a broad index fund. Bonds are boring, and that's a good thing. In my opinion, the stock market has more risk in a day than a high-quality bond fund has in a year. While the 2022 bond crash was epic, the 13% loss was statistically the equivalent of the stock market plunge during the Great Depression. And let's not forget the 1987 Black Monday stock crash when stocks lost over 20% in a single day. Bonds, on the other hand, gained that day — by about 50% more than bonds lost in the bond crash of 2022.

The iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF has earned a respectable 4.69% annually over the three years ending December 31, 2025. That pales in comparison to the 22.25% annual return of the iShares Core S&P Total U.S. Stock Market ETF. I predict that when stocks tank, you will be glad you have some boring bonds.

While the best bond substitute is often paying off debt, annuities and stocks of any kind are not bond substitutes. Nearly 70% of investment grade taxable bonds are issued and backed by the U.S. government. While I'm admittedly nervous about the impact increasing amounts of government debt will have on inflation, government-issued Treasury Inflation Protected Securities (TIPS) help protect from this possibility.

Allan Roth is founder of the planning firm Wealth Logic in Colorado Springs, Colo. He is a licensed CPA and CFP, and has an M.B.A. from Northwestern University (Kellogg), but still claims he can keep investing simple.

Editor's note: Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's Advisor newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to advisor.editors@barrons.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Intel's stock has been so strong that even skeptics have changed their minds
data.description:
By Hannah Pedone

The chip maker's stock is on track for the best month in at least 46 years - leading to no fewer than two analyst upgrades two days before it reports earnings

Intel's stock is up roughly 50% so far this month, putting it on pace for its best one-month percentage gain on record, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Intel's stock is on a tear - and as demand booms for its central processing units, analysts seen even more room for gains.

Shares of Intel have soared 50% so far in April, putting the stock on pace for its best-ever monthly gain based on data going back to 1980, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

That rally was enough to get skeptic BNP Paribas analyst David O'Connor to back away from his bearish call on the stock. While he hasn't yet flipped to bullish, O'Connor raised his rating on Intel shares to neutral Tuesday, ahead of the company's earnings report slated for Thursday. He noted that the "take-off" in agentic artificial intelligence is driving strong demand for the Intel's server chips.

Intel's stock was up 0.7% in recent afternoon trading Tuesday. It was just below its 26-year closing high of $68.50, seen on both April 16 and April 17.

O'Connor believes that Intel's progress on designing 14A - the company's next generation of chip technology, with a 1.4 nanometer processing node - has been garnering interest from customers and could translate to wins for the company in late 2026 and early 2027. He said that updates on 14A are likely to be a key driver for the stock through this year.

O'Connor noted that he previously held concerns over Intel's ability to compete with Arm Holdings (ARM) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) for market share on its server chips, but that concern has become less important as demand for Intel's CPUs grows due to hyperscalers vying for limited supplies.

However, in order to adopt a more bullish outlook, he said he would be looking to see progress in its foundry designs.

Meanwhile, HSBC's Frank Lee has seen enough to turn bullish. He upgraded the stock to buy from hold, and boosted his price target to a Wall Street high of $95, which implies 44% upside from current levels.

He sees "game-changing" potential for Intel's server CPUs starting this current quarter, amid overall foundry capacity constraints.

"[W]e continue to believe the server-CPU business is the key near-term catalyst to drive earnings upside," and that's still not priced into the stock even after the recent gains, Lee wrote in a note to clients.

Tigress Financial Partners analyst Ivan Feinseth, who reiterated his bullish view on Intel's stock, told MarketWatch that he's optimistic about the upcoming earnings results and added that they will most likely lead him to increase his 12-month price target. That target is currently $66, which is right around where the stock is now trading.

Feinseth said that Intel's recent partnerships have been catalysts for the stock in April. He noted that the early-April announcement of Intel's collaboration with Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) on central processing units for AI data centers, as well as its move to buy back Apollo Global Management's (APO) stake in a joint venture related to Intel's foundry in Ireland, had contributed to the stock moving higher this month.

Meanwhile, the U.S. government and Nvidia (NVDA) - which invested $8.9 billion and $5 billion, respectively, in Intel last year - are "two of the best anchor investors that you could have" in a company, he added, with those partnerships having given investors a much more favorable view of the stock.

Feinseth said the company is now "ahead of the AI curve" and he expects the stock to move "a lot higher" going forward.

-Hannah Pedone

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Steel Dynamics reports Q1 2026: $5.20B sales, $538M operating income, $403M net income
data.description:
Steel Dynamics reported first-quarter 2026 net sales of $5.20 billion and net income attributable to the company of $403.4 million (diluted EPS $2.78). Operating income was $538.0 million and adjusted EBITDA was $699.9 million for the quarter. The company generated cash flow from operations of $148.3 million while maintaining liquidity of $2.0 billion as of March 31, 2026.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: Net sales of $5,204,858 thousand for the three months ended March 31, 2026.
  • Gross profit: $763,223 thousand for the quarter.
  • Operating income: $538,004 thousand for the quarter.
  • Net income attributable to Steel Dynamics: $403,436 thousand for the quarter; diluted EPS $2.78.
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $699,897 thousand for the quarter.

Business Highlights

  • Record total steel shipments of 3.64 million tons for the quarter, driven by strength across flat roll and long product businesses.
  • Steel operations saw improved pricing and metal spread expansion; average external steel selling price rose to $1,193 per ton while average ferrous cost per ton melted was $396.
  • Continued commissioning and ramp-up of aluminum flat rolled sheet operations: finished product shipments increased to 22,500 metric tons and production qualified for beverage can and industrial customers; CASH line for automotive products produced qualification material.
  • Steel fabrication backlog expanded (over 38% higher year-over-year) with increased order activity across commercial, data center, manufacturing, warehouse, and healthcare sectors.
  • Metals recycling operating performance improved with higher ferrous and nonferrous selling values; shipments were slightly impacted by winter weather early in the quarter.

Original SEC Filing: STEEL DYNAMICS INC [ STLD ] - 8-K - Apr. 21, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Tim Cook's Apple Legacy Starts With the Stock, and Goes Way Beyond. The Story the Numbers Tell. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Steve Jobs and Tim Cook used their respective times as CEO of Apple differently, and each left behind his own legacy: Jobs is famed for introducing popular products while Cook has strategized ways to make those products generate massive returns for shareholders.

Apple announced on Monday night that Cook will step down as CEO in September. He will be replaced by John Ternus, who is currently Apple's senior vice president of hardware engineering.

Cook became CEO in August 2011 after Jobs announced his resignation. Jobs established himself as a strong leader who put Apple on the map and grew it into one of the most well-known consumer brands, and he is still referred to today as one of the most successful tech CEOs in history. That put pressure on Cook right from the start.

Jobs formed Apple with Steve Wozniak and Ronald Wayne in 1976. After some time away from the company, Jobs returned as interim CEO of Apple in 1997, and he officially became permanent CEO in 2000. Jobs didn't return to a flourishing business. It was the quick and savvy choices he made that helped the company, and its stock, eventually soar. Jobs was at the helm when Apple launched several culturally defining products, including the iPod, the iMac, and the iPad. The most well-known, of course, is the iPhone, which came out in 2007.

Apple stock surged 6,759% during Jobs' time as CEO, according to Dow Jones Market Data. On a compound annual growth rate basis, Apple sales increased by 21.5% annually under his leadership. Apple reported negative earnings the year Jobs came back. 2011 earnings were 99 cents per share.

Cook had big shoes to fill when he took over. The numbers show that his time as CEO will also be remembered as incredibly successful. Apple stock has soared 1,932% on a price return basis during Cook's nearly 15 years as CEO. That far outpaces the S&P 500, which has risen 504% in the same time frame. While that gain wasn't as high as when Jobs was at the helm, Cook took over a much more successful company than Jobs did.

Shares of PC competitor HP Inc. rose 71% in the same period, while BlackBerry, which sold an incredibly popular phone that lost its appeal after the iPhone's launch, fell 80% during Cook's tenure. Shares of Samsung Electronics jumped 1,415% and Lenovo Group gained 126% in that time as well.

Under Cook's leadership, Apple sales increased 10% annually and earnings per share grew 16% annually on a compound annual growth rate basis. Apple was the first U.S. company to hit a series of record market capitalizations: $1 trillion, in August 2018; $2 trillion, in August 2020; and $3 trillion, in June 2023. Apple now has a market cap of $4 trillion. ( Nvidia hit that last mark first, followed by Microsoft, Apple, and Alphabet.)

Cook also led the charge to boost Apple's services segment, including the launch of Apple Pay in 2014 and Apple Music in 2015. The services segment is now Apple's highest-margin business.

"Tim Cook has led Apple to focus on Services, broadened the product portfolio, and focused on health and privacy," BofA Securities analyst Wamsi Mohan wrote on Monday. "This in turn has driven massive shareholder returns under Cook's tenure."

Cook took a different approach than Jobs when it came to being Apple's CEO, but his methods worked well enough to push the company, and its stock, to new highs. That's worth remembering.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: T-Mobile Stock Falls on Report of Possible Deutsche Telekom Combination — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Janet H. Cho

Deutsche Telekom AG is weighing a merger with T-Mobile US, according to a report on Tuesday.

Shares of the German telecommunications provider fell 1.3% on the Bloomberg report, while shares of T-Mobile were up 0.2%.

Deutsche Telekom is a partner with T-Mobile and its largest shareholder, with 53% of the shares, according to FactSet.

T-Mobile could not immediately be reached for comment.

This is breaking news. Please check back for further updates.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Swiss Market Ends On Weak Note After Cautious Session
data.description:
BRUSSELS (dpa-AFX) - Swiss stocks ended notably lower on Tuesday after languishing in negative territory right through the day's session, as uncertainty about U.S.-Iran ceasefire rendered the mood cautious.

The benchmark SMI ended with a loss of 150.08 points or 1.13% at 13,134.14, off the day's low of 13,10759.

Lindt & Spruengli ended down 3.62% and Sandoz Group lost 3.1%. Lonza Group drifted lower by 2.85%.

Nestle, Novartis, UBS Group, Straumann Holding, Galderma Group and Givaudan lost 1.4%-2%. Sonova, Richemont, Alcon, Roche and Schindler Ps also closed notably lower.

Kuehne + Nagel climbed nearly 1.5%. Helvetia Baloise Holding, Swiss Re, VAT Group and Partners Group posted moderate gains.

In economic news, Switzerland's foreign trade surplus decreased at the end of the first quarter as exports fell amid an increase in imports, data from the Federal Customs Administration showed.

The trade balance registered a surplus of CHF 2.7 billion in March, down from CHF 4.3 billion in February. In real terms, exports fell 3.4% monthly in March, while imports climbed by 3.1%. Imports of chemical and pharmaceutical products alone surged 10%.

Nominal exports grew 1%, and imports logged a double-digit growth of 10.1%.

During the first quarter, the total trade surplus of the country was CHF 11.1 billion versus CHF 11.3 billion in the fourth quarter of 2025. In real terms, exports grew 3.7%, while imports were 1.2% lower.

data.title: Estee Lauder Engages JPMorgan For 5 Billion Puig Deal Financing
data.description:
Estee Lauder appears to be moving closer to a transformative deal, with JPMorgan Chase now taking a central role in structuring a roughly 5 billion ($5.9 billion) financing package tied to a potential combination with Puig Brands (PUGBY). The bank is reportedly coordinating discussions with other lenders to support what could become a cash-and-stock takeover bid, suggesting that talks may be shifting from exploratory discussions toward a more defined transaction framework.

This financing effort follows earlier indications that negotiations between Estee Lauder and Puig are advancing, with both companies already confirming in March that discussions were underway. People familiar with the matter have indicated the deal could lean heavily on stock, a structure that may help align valuation expectations while maintaining flexibility for both sides. The potential combination would bring together a portfolio of high-end brands including MAC, Le Labo, Charlotte Tilbury, and Byredo, potentially creating a broader and more globally competitive platform in luxury beauty.

If completed, the transaction could significantly reshape the competitive landscape, positioning the combined entity as a more credible challenger to L'Oreal. While key details such as final structure and timing remain uncertain, the emergence of a large-scale financing plan may indicate increasing momentum behind the discussions. For investors, the situation could be worth monitoring closely, as execution, integration, and scale benefits will likely determine whether the deal delivers long-term value.

data.title: QUOTE BOX--Investor reactions to Senate hearing for Fed chair candidate Kevin Warsh
data.description:
Markets were lower on Tuesday as President Donald Trump's candidate for the next chair of the Federal Reserve, Kevin Warsh, appeared before the U.S. Senate for a confirmation hearing.

Warsh's appearance was being watched by investors and analysts because he has publicly questioned the Fed's role in markets and has said he would seek to shrink the central bank's balance sheet. Warsh promised to collaborate with the administration to keep the U.S. dollar strong while calling for "regime change" that would include a new approach to controlling inflation.

The question-and-answer session was overshadowed to some extent by news that Iran hadn't confirmed it would attend a second round of peace talks with the U.S. — a development late in the New York morning that sent oil prices higher and short-circuited a modest rally in U.S. stock indexes.

Here are reactions to Warsh's appearance before the Senate on Tuesday:

MARK HACKETT, CHIEF MARKET STRATEGIST, NATIONWIDE INVESTMENT MANAGEMENT GROUP, PHILADELPHIA:

“The market has been softening and rates rising through the hearing, mostly due to the discussion on quantitative easing. There has been uncertainty on which direction the Fed with head based on his previous statements. This, combined from some exhaustion from the 9% month to date rally and uncertainty around earnings season, is causing a well-earned and likely temporary pause in the equity market.”

WALTER TODD, PRESIDENT, CHIEF INVESTMENT OFFICER, GREENWOOD CAPITAL, GREENWOOD, SOUTH CAROLINA:

"It's a lot of typical political positioning by the people asking the questions. And I think Warsh has done a pretty good job of trying to bring the conversation back to monetary policy.

"His discussion that's happening right now around the balance sheet is very interesting, and on the surface, it may be taken as a net negative for markets if they believe he's going to follow through. He's talking about doing it deliberately and slowly and over many years and all these things.

“But the concept that the Fed under his leadership is going to move away from using the balance sheet as an instrument to implement policy would be quite a significant change from the framework we've seen since the financial crisis."

NOEL DIXON, SENIOR MACRO STRATEGIST, STATE STREET, BOSTON:

"With Warsh, you’re going to have a dovish leaning Fed. I think there would be a coalition within the Board of Governors – (Michelle) Bowman, (Christopher) Waller, (Stephen) Miran and then Kevin Warsh and that’s four out of seven Board of Governors – that will be leaning dovish.

"There is also (John) Williams, who's very sensitive to stock market activity. So if the markets aren't happy with the fact that we don't get cuts, and you get an adverse reaction in tech companies, Warsh is going to react. So there is a coalition within the Fed Board that would possibly put some cuts through, maybe a cut or two by the end of the year. I think that is still on the table."

"The other thing that struck me was when a senator asked him if he would lower rates to 1% — I guess Trump had indicated that he would like to have rates below 2% — Warsh didn't really say no to that. He didn't say that it would increase prices. He kind of leaned on it and said there would be a lagged effect, and he was just very non committal to that. So it's almost like — just reading between the lines — he's giving himself space to maintain possible justification for rate cuts by the end of the year."

ERIC PARNELL, CHIEF MARKET STRATEGIST AT GVA WEALTH MANAGEMENT, BERWYN, PENNSYLVANIA:

"The activity out of Washington is always worthwhile to watch because there's a theatrical aspect to it, of course, in terms of making sure that the policy initiatives that different politicians want to express, this is their opportunity to do that.

"We've seen this before. We're likely to see it again in the future. Once the positions have been established and priorities have been set in terms of congressional oversight and of the whole process and how the Fed's going to move forward, it's our expectations that from the middle of May that we'll be transitioning in an orderly way to a new Fed chair.

"One of the more important things that we'll want to watch as we go through the second half of the year is that there's been a lot of investor questions around the independence of the Fed going forward and how the Fed's going to handle the balance sheet as well as interest rate policy."

THOMAS MARTIN, SENIOR PORTFOLIO MANAGER, GLOBALT INVESTMENTS, ATLANTA:

"He's known to be opportunistic. And so if he wants the job, which, it seems like he wants the job, then you have to know that you're going to have to say the right thing. Otherwise, you're not going to get offered the job. So then you have to say the right thing without looking like you're saying the right thing when you're going to get asked about that anyway. And so he's making his case, I guess, on other grounds, or whatever grounds he thinks are going to make sense - AI, nice candidate - that rates can be structurally lower.

"But then on the other hand, he has said that he believes the Fed needs to be independent and they need to make their own path. I don't think anybody thinks he's a bad choice. I mean, maybe the Democrats do. I don't know who else they would want, but the issue is Tom Tillis is not going to vote in favor of proceeding until Trump drops the suit against the Fed and against Powell. So it's kind of moot until there's a resolution on that front.

"And, then after that does get resolved, then the chairman has a lot of influence, but everybody else has to vote. And they'll be happy to tell you that they are independent and they will vote the way they want to vote. I understand that job is to build that consensus, whatever you think it might be, I'm sure that the chair thinks they have an idea of what they want it to be, and they try and move people in that direction, but I don't know that they really can make people vote in a certain way. I think the committee is going to be fairly skeptical of Warsh anyway, as a new guy and as having a little bit moved around in his position as to what he thinks is the right thing to do."

data.title: US dollar climbs as uncertainty grows over Iran ceasefire
data.description:
Key points:
  • Iran undecided on attending peace talks after US forces board oil tanker
  • Fed chief nominee Kevin Warsh urges central bank overhaul in Senate testimony
  • US retail sales jump as higher gasoline prices and tax refunds boost consumer spending

By Karen Brettell

The U.S. dollar reached a one-week high on Tuesday as concerns grew that a ceasefire in the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran will take longer to reach and lead to prolonged disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran said on Tuesday it had yet to decide whether to attend the peace talks after U.S. forces boarded a huge Iranian oil tanker at sea with just a day left before the ceasefire runs out in the war in the Gulf.

“Investors are bracing for a more prolonged disruption in the Strait of Hormuz as US-Iran negotiations show signs of grinding to a standstill," said Karl Schamotta, chief market strategist at Corpay in Toronto.

"The two sides remain far apart on key terms, and a ceasefire deadline expires tomorrow," he said.

Markets have been swayed in recent days by contradictory headlines over whether or not U.S. Vice President JD Vance has departed for Pakistan for the talks.

Vance has not yet departed Washington for talks on Iran and is participating in additional policy meetings, a White House official said on Tuesday.

"We felt like maybe the Kumbaya moment was going to come, and it was going to be announced and then you open the Strait," said Juan Perez, director of trading at Monex USA in Washington. "That hasn't happened and that's what markets really want."

The dollar has fallen this month on growing optimism of a ceasefire in the war, after gaining last month on safe haven demand due to the conflict.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, was last up 0.36% at 98.43, with the euro down 0.44% at $1.1736.

The Japanese yen weakened 0.37% against the greenback to 159.39 per dollar. Sterling weakened 0.29% to $1.3493.

Federal Reserve chief nominee Kevin Warsh, meanwhile, called for "regime change" at the U.S. central bank, including a new approach for controlling inflation and a communications overhaul that may discourage his colleagues from saying too much about the direction of monetary policy.

Warsh made the comments in testimony before the U.S. Senate.

The dollar was also supported earlier on Tuesday after U.S. retail sales increased more than expected in March as the war with Iran boosted gasoline prices and led to a record surge in receipts at service stations, while tax refunds underpinned spending elsewhere.

“The data in the U.S. is telling a consistent story of a decent acceleration,” said Adam Button, chief currency analyst at investingLive. “That's been obscured by the war and it should be a U.S. dollar tailwind because I find it hard to believe that we will go back to pricing in two cuts this year.”

Futures traders are pricing in a 30% chance the Fed makes one 25-basis-point cut this year.

data.title: Casey Wasserman's Talent Agency Draws Takeover Interest — WSJ
data.description:
By Jessica Toonkel and Lauren Thomas

The sale of Casey Wasserman's talent and marketing agency following the release of emails between him and Jeffrey Epstein associate Ghislaine Maxwell drew interest from private-equity firms and big Hollywood investors.

Suitors include the investment firms Permira, New Mountain Capital and TA Associates, United Talent Agency and former Endeavor chair Patrick Whitesell, according to people familiar with the matter.

A potential deal could involve all or part of the agency, which could be valued at around $3 billion, some of the people said. The process is still in its early stages and the list of prospective buyers is likely to be whittled down in the coming weeks, the people added.

The deal marks an opportunity for buyers to nab a stake in an agency that works with A-list musicians including Kendrick Lamar and Kacey Musgraves as well as a roster of athletes across major sports leagues.

Wasserman announced in February that he was selling the firm, which he renamed The Team from Wasserman in March. Clients had defected after the Justice Department released documents earlier this year that included flirtatious 2003 emails between him and Maxwell.

Wasserman, who founded the firm 20 years ago and owns a minority stake in it, has the right to veto any sale. Providence Equity Partners owns about 60% and has told people the company would be willing to roll over its equity in the event that a party only buys Wasserman's stake, people familiar with the matter said.

A potential sale would represent one of the biggest repercussions for a Hollywood executive as a result of the release of the Epstein files.

The company's annual earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization are around $200 million, some of the people familiar with the matter said. Initial nonbinding bids were due Monday as part of a process managed by the boutique investment bank Moelis & Co.

The New York Times earlier reported that Permira, UTA and Whitesell were among the parties that had expressed interest in the firm.

In 2002, Wasserman and his then-wife flew on Epstein's private jet as part of a trip to visit HIV/AIDS project sites in Africa with Maxwell, Epstein, former President Bill Clinton and others. One of the attendees on the trip, an emergency physician, told federal agents in a 2020 interview that several young women — including a masseuse, a model and a ballerina — were also on the trip for reasons he couldn't understand.

Wasserman issued an apology after his emails with Maxwell were released and told staff he regretted sending them. He has acknowledged the 2002 flight, but said he had "never had a personal or business relationship with Jeffrey Epstein" and that the trip was "years before their criminal conduct came to light."

The Clintons have said they had no personal knowledge of Epstein's criminal activities and the former president cut ties with the convicted sex offender more than two decades ago.

In the aftermath of the release, a number of clients, including Grammy winner Chappell Roan and U.S. women's soccer star Abby Wambach said they were leaving Wasserman's agency.

Wasserman is continuing in his role as chairman of the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics after the board of the committee engaged outside counsel to review his past interactions with Epstein and Maxwell and found that it didn't go further than what had been publicly documented.

Write to Jessica Toonkel at jessica.toonkel@wsj.com and Lauren Thomas at lauren.thomas@wsj.com

data.title: BP Faces Shareholder Backlash Despite Shares Reaching 16-Year High
data.description:
BP Plc entered its annual meeting with momentum that could signal a turning point after several difficult years, supported by a new chairman, a new chief executive officer, and a strategy shift that is starting to gain traction. With shares trading at a 16-year high, the market appears to be responding to the company's move back toward hydrocarbons after a period marked by weaker returns from renewable investments. However, what might have been a straightforward endorsement of that progress has instead developed into a more contentious situation around governance and shareholder engagement.

The tension emerged after Follow This, a Dutch climate-focused investor group, proposed that BP and Shell Plc outline financial plans for scenarios where global oil and gas demand declines. While Shell agreed to put the proposal to a vote, BP declined, citing what it considers a technical issue with how the resolution was structured. That decision has drawn criticism from some shareholders, including Legal & General Investment Management, which plans to vote against the re-election of chairman Albert Manifold. Other investors across the UK and US are also expected to oppose him. Even investors who broadly support BP's strategic direction have suggested the company may have mishandled the situation, particularly given expectations around transparency and responsiveness.

For investors, the episode could be notable because it echoes earlier periods when resistance to external pressure coincided with value destruction, multiple CEO changes, and a strategy that shifted direction over time. The company's recent improvement may partly reflect pressure from activist investor Elliott Investment Management LP, making the current dispute a potential test of whether governance practices are evolving alongside strategy. BP's renewed focus on hydrocarbons could continue to support earnings and cash flow, particularly if oil prices remain favorable, but ongoing governance friction may weigh on sentiment. At this stage, the company could be seen as investable again, though the durability of that view may depend on both execution and how management engages with dissenting shareholders.

data.title: Corn, soy gain on technical buying as rain seen slowing Midwest planting
data.description:
Key points:
  • USDA rates winter wheat crop below analyst expectations
  • Rain forecast for central US may stall planting of corn, soy
  • Rising oil prices and US-China trade hopes also support grain markets

By Karl Plume

U.S. corn and soybean futures firmed on Tuesday on technical buying and higher oil prices, and as rain forecast for the Midwest this week was poised to slow planting that has been slightly ahead of the normal pace.

New-crop December corn futures (CZ26) on the Chicago Board of Trade touched a two-week high while November soybeans (SX26) hit a 5-1/2-week high.

Wheat futures advanced after the U.S. Department of Agriculture's weekly crop condition rating fell below trade expectations amid a harsh drought in parts of the Plains wheat belt.

Rising crude oil prices underpinned grain markets after U.S. President Donald Trump said he did not want to extend a soon-to-expire ceasefire in the Iran war. Oil prices can influence grains as corn and soybeans are feedstocks for biofuels, and disruptions to fertilizer supplies due to the Strait of Hormuz closure may affect farming costs and yields.

Investors are also looking ahead to a mid-May meeting between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping amid hopes China will agree to additional purchases of U.S. soybeans and other agricultural goods.

"President Trump had some positive comments about Xi in recent days, including this morning. And I also think there are a few stories about biofuel, which are getting a little more interest as well," said Rich Nelson, chief strategist with Allendale.

CBOT May corn (CK26) settled up 1-3/4 cents at $4.53-3/4 a bushel after breaking through technical chart resistance at its 20-day moving average and rising above the prior-session high. May soybeans (SK26) held technical support at its 20- and 50-day moving averages, and rose 8-3/4 cents to $11.74-1/2 a bushel, drawing strength from a 3.5% jump in soyoil futures (BOv1).

CBOT July wheat (WN26) gained 6-3/4 cents to $6.12-3/4 a bushel, while K.C. July wheat (KWN26) rose 9 cents to $6.56-1/2 a bushel.

The USDA on Monday rated 30% of the nation's winter wheat in good-to-excellent condition, down from 34% the previous week and below a range of analyst estimates.

data.title: Stocks fall, oil gains on growing doubts over Iran peace talks
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S. President Donald Trump says he does not want to extend Iran ceasefire
  • Fed nominee Warsh calls for 'regime change' at Senate Banking Committee hearing
  • US retail sales data breezes past analyst expectations

By Stephen Culp

Wall Street stocks followed their global counterparts lower on Tuesday and crude prices extended their gains, as optimism over peace talks faded and the U.S.-Iran ceasefire expiration loomed.

All three major U.S. stock indexes reversed earlier gains, while gold dropped and the dollar advanced.

Iran said it had yet to decide whether to send a delegation to Islamabad for eleventh-hour peace talks, after U.S. forces seized a huge Iranian oil tanker in international waters to enforce President Donald Trump's blockade.

"What's happening in the Middle East is the primary market driver of the very short term, and that very short term can be minute-to-minute," said Chuck Carlson, chief executive officer at Horizon Investment Services in Hammond, Indiana. "I'm not surprised by the way the market is kind of behaving with that deadline looming near."

WARSH FACES THE SENATE

Kevin Warsh, U.S. President Donald Trump's pick to succeed Jerome Powell as Federal Reserve chair, called for a "regime change" at the central bank in comments before the Senate Banking Committee, in which he called for a new approach to controlling inflation and a communications overhaul that could discourage his colleagues from saying too much about the direction of monetary policy.

A report from the Commerce Department showed U.S. retail sales were more robust than analysts expected in March, but much of the upside surprise was driven by a 15.5% surge in gasoline station receipts due to price spikes related to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 214.93 points, or 0.43%, to 49,227.63, the S&P 500 fell 32.47 points, or 0.46%, to 7,076.67 and the Nasdaq Composite fell 94.73 points, or 0.39%, to 24,309.86.

EUROPEAN, GLOBAL STOCKS DIP

European shares ended lower as risk appetite soured ahead of the ceasefire deadline.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI fell 4.75 points, or 0.44%, to 1,067.24.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index fell 0.87%, while Europe's broad FTSEurofirst 300 index fell 22.64 points, or 0.91%.

Emerging market stocks CBOE:EFS rose 12.09 points, or 0.76%, to 1,612.47. MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) closed higher by 0.87%, to 825.46, while Japan's Nikkei rose 524.28 points, or 0.89%, to 59,349.17.

The U.S. dollar edged higher on optimism over Iran war negotiations, while the retail sales report signalled economic strength.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, rose 0.38% to 98.44, with the euro down 0.45% at $1.1734.

Against the Japanese yen , the dollar strengthened 0.4% to 159.42.

In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin fell 1.08% to $75,490.88. Ethereum declined 1.38% to $2,306.21.

Oil prices reversed an earlier dip after Trump said that while he hopes for a "great deal," he did not want to extend the ceasefire.

U.S. crude rose 2.81% to settle at $92.13 per barrel, while Brent settled at $98.48 per barrel, up 3.14% on the day.

U.S. Treasury yields rose after retail sales data reinforced expectations that the Fed will keep rates steady this year.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes rose 4.5 basis points to 4.296%, from 4.25% late on Monday.

The 30-year bond (US30YT=RR) yield rose 2.2 basis points to 4.903% from 4.881% late on Monday.

The 2-year note (US2YT=RR) yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Federal Reserve, rose 6.7 basis points to 3.783%, from 3.716% late on Monday.

Gold prices dropped as the dollar firmed, as investors looked to tentative U.S.-Iran talks and Warsh's Senate confirmation hearing.

Spot gold fell 2.46% to $4,700.89 an ounce. U.S. gold futures (GCc1) fell 2.15% to $4,703.40 an ounce.

data.title: RedChip's Biotech Virtual Investor Conference Replays Now Available
data.description:
ORLANDO, FL / ACCESS Newswire / April 21, 2026 / RedChip Companies, an industry leader in investor relations, media, and research for microcap and small-cap companies, today announced that on-demand replays from its Biotech Resurgence: Platforms and Pipelines of Today's Innovators investor conference, held April 16, 2026, are now available.

The full-day virtual event featured senior executives from publicly traded companies advancing innovation across biotechnology, therapeutics, medical devices, diagnostics, and digital health. Investors who were unable to attend the live event-or who wish to revisit specific company presentations-can now access full video replays at their convenience.

"The strong engagement we saw throughout this conference-drawing nearly 100,000 viewers-highlights the continued investor focus on innovation across the healthcare sector," said Dave Gentry, CEO of RedChip Companies. "From clinical-stage drug development to scalable healthcare platforms, the presenting companies demonstrated the breadth of opportunity emerging across biotech and healthcare. By making these presentations available on demand, we are extending access to valuable insights into companies driving the next wave of healthcare innovation."

Conference Presentation Replays

Investors may view individual company presentations and Q&A sessions using the links below:

  • IN8bio (Nasdaq:INAB)

  • DiaMedica Therapeutics (Nasdaq:DMAC)

  • Nasus Pharma (Nasdaq:NSRX)

  • Calidi Biotherapeutics (NYSE American:CLDI)

Additional presentation replays are available on RedChip's YouTube channel.

Each replay includes a management presentation followed by a live investor Q&A session, offering deeper insight into clinical development strategies, pipeline progress, commercialization pathways, and competitive positioning across the healthcare landscape.

The Biotech Resurgence conference was designed to provide institutional and retail investors with direct access to companies developing next-generation therapies, enabling technologies, and scalable healthcare solutions. As highlighted throughout the event, innovation across biotech and healthcare continues to accelerate, driven by advances in science, technology, and care delivery models.

About RedChip Companies

RedChip Companies, an Inc. 5000 company, is an international investor relations, media, and research firm focused on microcap and small-cap companies. Founded in 1992 as a small-cap research firm, RedChip gained early recognition for initiating coverage on emerging blue chip companies such as Apple, Starbucks, Daktronics, Winnebago, and Nike. Over the past 33 years, RedChip has evolved into a full-service investor relations and media firm, delivering concrete, measurable results for its clients, which have included U.S. Steel, Perfumania, Cidara Therapeutics, and Celsius Holdings, among others. Our newsletter, Small Stocks, Big Money™, is delivered online weekly to 60,000 investors. RedChip has developed the most comprehensive service platform in the industry for microcap and small-cap companies. These services include the following: a worldwide distribution network for its stock research; retail and institutional roadshows in major U.S. cities; outbound marketing to stock brokers, RIAs, institutions, and family offices; a digital media investor relations platform that has generated millions of unique investor views; investor webinars and group calls; a television show, Small Stocks, Big Money™, which airs weekly on Bloomberg US; TV commercials in local and national markets; corporate and product videos; website design; and traditional investor relation services, which include press release writing, development of investor presentations, quarterly conference call script writing, strategic consulting, capital raising, and more.

Sign Up for RedChat

RedChat™ is an AI-powered investment research assistant designed to give investors instant access to critical insights from SEC filings, press releases, and corporate disclosures. Built to streamline small-cap and microcap stock research, RedChat analyzes thousands of public company documents and delivers clear, context-rich answers to investor questions in seconds. Instead of manually reviewing lengthy filings, investors can simply ask RedChat about financial results, partnerships, business strategy, or recent announcements and receive precise, source-based summaries. Investors can experience RedChat and start exploring stocks today at www.redchip.com/stocks or www.red.chat.

To learn more about RedChip's products and services, please visit:

"Discovering Tomorrow's Blue Chips Today"™

Follow RedChip on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/company/redchip/

Follow RedChip on Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/RedChipCompanies

data.title: JD Sports Fashion chair Andy Higginson to step down, Sky News says
data.description:
Retail veteran Andy Higginson will step down as the chairman of British sportswear retailer JD Sports Fashion , Sky News reported on Tuesday.

An announcement regarding the exit could be made imminently, the report said, adding there had been no indication that Higginson was preparing to leave the firm.

JD declined to comment on the report.

Higginson, who joined the firm in 2022, had previously served as chair of supermarket group Morrisons and also held senior roles at supermarket chain Tesco.
